title: The g Family
subtitle: 30+ commands hide behind the g prefix.
summary: g is a meta prefix: it gates a second, hidden keyboard of commands. From gd (go to definition) and gf (go to file) to gq (reformat) and gv (re-select), the g family covers everything that didn't fit elsewhere.

blocks.text: The {key:g} prefix is Vim's *miscellaneous drawer* — and it's huge. Roughly thirty commands live behind it. The unifying theme is "this didn't fit on a one-key shortcut, so it got the {key:g} prefix." Worth a flat reference table.

blocks.title: Why So Many g Commands
blocks.text: Vim has more useful operations than there are unmodified keys. The {key:g} prefix gives a second keyboard's worth of room. Some of the {key:g} family are *operators* themselves ({key:g}{key:U}, {key:g}{key:u}, {key:g}{key:~}, {key:g}{key:q}, {key:g}{key:@}); some are *motions* ({key:g}{key:e}, {key:g}{key:E}, {key:g}{key:j}, {key:g}{key:k}, {key:g}{key:0}, {key:g}{key:^}, {key:g}{key:_}); some are *jumps* ({key:g}{key:d}, {key:g}{key:f}, {key:g}{key:;}, {key:g}{key:,}, {key:g}{key:i}); some are one-shot actions ({key:g}{key:v}, {key:g}{key:a}, {key:g}{key:x}, {key:g}{key:8}).

rows: Go to local definition under cursor
Open file under cursor at line :{n}
Back to end of previous word
Back to end of previous WORD
Open URL under cursor in browser
Like p/P, but cursor lands AFTER pasted text

blocks.text: If you only learn a handful: {key:g}{key:v}, {key:g}{key:i}, {key:g}{key:;}, {key:g}{key:U}{key:i}{key:w}, {key:g}{key:f}, {key:g}{key:x}, {key:g}{key:j}/{key:g}{key:k}. Those alone justify the prefix.

blocks.title: {key:g} is a prefix router, not a command
blocks.text: Vim's parser is a prefix dispatch tree: after {key:g} it waits for a second key and dispatches to a different handler depending on what arrives ({key:g}{key:g}, {key:g}{key:e}, {key:g}{key:U}, {key:g}{key:;}, …). That's why {key:g} alone does nothing — it's an open node in the tree. Same pattern for {key:z}, {key:[}, {key:]}, and the operator-pending state.
